title: EU Data Residency & Sovereignty Baseline
notes: Maps Regionlock enforcement checks to the specific legal provisions they provide evidence of controls for. This is evidence of technical/organizational controls (Art. 32) and placement enforcement, not a cryptographic proof that data never physically left the EU.

rules.name: Workloads pinned to an EU region
rules.description: Every workload declares node placement constrained to an allow-listed EU cloud region via topology.kubernetes.io/region.
rules.rationale: Unconstrained or non-EU placement can schedule pods (and the data they process) outside the EEA, constituting a third-country transfer.

articles.title: Transfers on the basis of an adequacy decision
International governmental access and transfer of non-personal data

rules.name: No unrestricted or extra-EU egress
rules.description: Services do not proxy to external endpoints (ExternalName) and NetworkPolicies do not permit unrestricted (0.0.0.0/0, ::/0) egress that could reach non-EU destinations.
rules.rationale: Open egress or external-name proxying can move personal data to endpoints outside the EEA without a transfer safeguard.

rules.name: Storage uses customer-managed keys
rules.description: PersistentVolumeClaims are annotated with a customer-managed key (CMK) reference, so the data controller (not only the cloud provider) controls the encryption keys.
rules.rationale: Customer-managed keys reduce provider access to plaintext and strengthen the sovereignty posture required for sensitive EU data.

rules.name: Encryption at rest declared
rules.description: PersistentVolumeClaims declare encryption at rest via the configured label/annotation.
rules.rationale: Encryption at rest is a baseline technical measure expected under the security-of-processing obligation.
